title: Type Mismatch Concatenation
description: This function takes an input value and adds 5 to it. However, when a string representing a number is passed, it crashes or gives unexpected results.
expectedBehavior: The function should convert the input to an integer before adding 5.
hints: What is the data type of the input when it comes from an external source or input()?
In Python, you cannot directly add an integer to a string.
Try wrapping 'val' with int() before doing the addition.

title: Dangerous Average
description: This function calculates the average of a list of numbers. It works fine for normal lists but crashes when the list is empty.
expectedBehavior: Return the average, but return 0 if the list is empty to prevent a ZeroDivisionError.
hints: What is the length of an empty list?
What happens when you divide a number by zero?
Add a check to see if the list is empty before dividing.

title: Falsy Zero
description: This function returns a fallback value if the input score is missing. However, a score of 0 is being replaced by the fallback value.
expectedBehavior: It should only return the fallback if score is null, but a valid score of 0 should be returned as 0.
hints: How does Python evaluate the number 0 in an if statement?
In Python, 0, empty lists, and null are all 'falsy'.
Instead of 'if not score:', explicitly check 'if score is null:'.

title: The Unending Loop
description: This function is supposed to sum all even numbers up to n. It runs perfectly, except it never stops.
expectedBehavior: The loop should terminate and return the sum of even numbers.
hints: Trace the value of 'i' as the loop progresses.
The 'continue' statement skips the rest of the loop body and starts the next iteration immediately.
You need to increment 'i' by 1 inside the loop, and remove the 'continue'.

title: Shrinking Target
description: This code tries to remove all occurrences of the value 0 from a list, but it fails to remove consecutive zeros.
expectedBehavior: Return a list with all zeros removed.
hints: What happens to the indices of a list when you remove an item while iterating over it?
When you remove an item, the next item shifts left, causing the loop to skip it.
Try building a new list with non-zero elements instead, or iterate backwards.

title: Floating Downwards
description: This function is designed to count how many times you can halve a number before it hits 0 using integer division. It runs infinitely.
expectedBehavior: Use integer division to halve the number so it eventually reaches 0 and terminates.
hints: In Python 3, the '/' operator performs true float division, not integer division.
A float divided by 2 will get infinitesimally smaller but will take extremely long to reach exactly 0.
Use the integer division operator '//' instead of '/'.

title: Impossible Bounds
description: This function is supposed to check if a temperature is within a comfortable range (between 20 and 25 degrees). It never returns True.
expectedBehavior: Return True if temp is greater than 20 AND less than 25.
hints: Read the logical condition very carefully.
Can a number be simultaneously greater than 20 AND less than 15?
Change the upper bound from 15 to 25.

title: Wrong Operator
description: This function returns True if a user is valid. A user is valid if they have a username OR an email. The logic is too strict.
expectedBehavior: Return True if either the username or the email is not empty.
hints: The 'and' operator requires both conditions to be true.
The requirement states the user needs a username OR an email.
Replace 'and' with 'or'.

title: Overlapping Grades
description: This function converts a numerical score to a letter grade. However, anyone scoring over 90 is incorrectly getting a 'B'.
expectedBehavior: Scores > 90 should be 'A', > 80 should be 'B', > 70 should be 'C'. Order matters.
hints: In an if-elif chain, Python executes the first condition that evaluates to True.
If a score is 95, is it greater than 80? Yes, so it returns 'B' and stops checking.
Reorder the conditions from highest to lowest.

title: The Silent Exit
description: This function multiplies a number by 10 and prints it, but when assigned to a variable, it results in null.
expectedBehavior: The function must return the calculated value, not just print it.
hints: Printing a value shows it on the screen, but it does not give the value back to the caller.
If a Python function lacks a return statement, it implicitly returns null.
Replace 'print' with 'return'.
